title: 保持终端的相关性：AI 代理驱动的命令行界面模式
author: Sriram Madapusi Vasudevan
content:
Key Takeaways

  • Well-designed CLIs are crucial in the agentic AI era, serving both human users and autonomous agents with precision and reliability.
  • Ensure every CLI command has a machine friendly escape hatch: Flags, environment variables, and semantic exit codes allow for automation compatibility while preventing and at times helping with agent failures.
  • Treat CLI output formats as stable API contracts. Breaking changes to any structured outputs can disrupt all automation workflows; semantic version releases and validate CLI output schemas on every change.
  • Prioritize adoption of the MCP protocol for agent integration from day one. This enables dynamic capability discovery, thereby allowing tools to be immediately usable by AI agents.
  • Tighten feedback loops with real-time feedback and graceful termination, because tokens are currency for AI agents.

In 2019, version 2 of the AWS CLI changed the default pager to less. This change serves as a cautionary tale for the future, where breaking changes are even more critical with the rise of AI agents. On that day, thousands of CI jobs failed because the pager had become interactive, waiting for keyboard input that would not show up in headless environments. Yes, it was human-fixable through configuration. But this event highlights a foundational principle in the world of AI agents: CLIs must work reliably without requiring intervention.

I’ve watched the transformation from human-centric workflows to agentic ones accelerate dramatically in recent years. Tools like GitHub Copilot CLI and Warp Agent Mode translate natural language into command sequences that need to execute reliably. Today, all popular LLM providers support multi-step, chained agentic workflows. The shift is real.

After maintaining CLIs for the better part of a decade, I’d like to dive into five design principles that will position CLI tools for success in this AI era.

Human Conveniences Need Escape Hatches

The AWS CLI pager incident illustrates the core challenge. Agents can’t answer prompts, press keys to navigate pagers or even interpret color in the output. The commands they run need to be deterministic in order to gauge the next course of operation.

The patterns that work provide three different escape routes.

Explicit Flags

Create explicit flags such as --no-prompt or --no-interactive that would disable reads from stdin on all CLI commands. There would be no confirmation prompts or pagers. These are simple changes, but are discoverable by humans through --help text and usable in automation for deterministic behavior.

Environment Variables

Environment variables allow for a one-time override, but establish global context and control before any commands are run. e.g., NO_COLOR=true removes colorization across all CLI outputs. Reserve your CLI’s tool name as the prefix for custom environment variables such as MYCLI_PROFILE=dev to enable tool context. These CLI-specific environment variables should be documented in the "Environment" section of the --help text to enable quick discoverability. Establish a clear precedence model: explicit flags > env vars to promote determinism.

Semantic Exit Codes

Exit codes relay deeper information about command execution. Emit 0 for success, 1-2 for correctable user defined errors and 3-125 for application specific errors. As with environment variables, exit codes need to be completely documented and kept stable across minor versions.

But here’s what I learned working with AI agents: Agents exhibit different behavioral usage patterns than humans. They chain commands together in quick sequences where one command’s output is piped to another command’s input. Multiple operations are spun up in parallel rather than sequentially as humans do. They even retry based on parsing structured output information and course-correct to get to the final destination. AI agents also look for multiple ways to confirm their work, such as running subsequent commands once done, to check for success rather than trusting exit codes alone. This approach indicates that operations need to be idempotent and state changes must be observable through follow-up commands.

These patterns are not hard to implement, but require attention to coherence. Create consistent global flag patterns that allow for structured output on all commands such as --output json and ensure your CLI has commands to check status of resource(s) it owns.

Output formats are an API contract

Here’s something that is not fully considered: Every CLI tool that outputs structured data is publishing an API contract. It does not matter if its JSON/YAML or TOML-structured output leads to downstream consumers depending on the output field names, data types, or even where the output is emitted, be it stderr or stdout.

Let’s consider two examples, kubectl and terraform.

Kubernetes deprecated a flag in v1.14 (--export) and later removed it in v1.18. The flag allowed cluster-specific metadata and moving/exporting to be stripped to a new cluster. The removal was due to the inherent feature having multiple bugs and not covering the full surface area of services it needed to support. Such a deprecation is reasonable from the perspective of a CLI maintainer.

However, the community response told a different story. Helm charts that used --export for template generation were broken. CI/CD pipelines exporting resources for backup or checking them into version control stopped working. Doing a GitHub search today still reveals thousands of references to kubectl get --export.

Because the deprecation was well-documented, this deprecation wasn’t simply a communication failure. The failure was not recognizing that CLI output formats are contracts and contracts have dependencies. In day-to-day use, humans can adapt and find creative workarounds to such problems. Automated systems in this situation, however, fail catastrophically, with no easy way to recover.

Terraform goes about it a different way. Every terraform state file explicitly includes version information.This inclusion provides a safer schema evolution while prioritizing backward compatibility.

{
  "terraform_version": "1.5.0",
  "serial": 1,
  "lineage": "3c77e9e7-0d23-9db4-4b42-4e12d0d531b4",
  "version": 4
}

The terraform state file is a live artifact that lives through the initialization, first write, and subsequent updates through the terraform CLI. Usually, newer releases of the terraform CLI can transparently upgrade an older state. Running either against an older JSON schema-encoded version or older terraform CLI version explicitly errors out, preventing corruption. Finally, lineage even tracks remote state vs. local state, preventing accidental overwrites.

As evidenced above, the solution here requires treating CLI outputs as a "versioned" API surface area. For all commands that produce structured outputs, it is highly beneficial to define explicit schemas using JSON schema or CUE. Even better, make them part of your CI infrastructure to detect breakages before they are out in the wild.

name: ci-check
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  validate:
    steps:
      - make install # install mycli
      - make generate-schema # generate schema.cue
      - run: mycli show --output json > show-output.json
      - run: cue vet schema.cue show-output.json # detect breaking change

In conclusion, treat CLI outputs just like APIs and follow similar evolution patterns. Additive changes are always safe, such as new, optional fields or new commands. However, breaking changes should require major version bumps. Rely on semantic versioning, but if changes are unavoidable, ensure migration paths are baked into the tooling itself to ease the transition. Better yet, architect your CLI in such a way that breaking changes do not affect automated workflows due to client-side versioning of any state that is created by your CLI.

Expose CLI capabilities via MCP

Model Context Protocol represents one of the most significant ways in which LLM-driven AI agents can now integrate with custom CLIs. Instead of agents being expected to know about the tools based on training data, they can discover capabilities dynamically and execute CLIs through a constrained and versioned schema.

Importantly, without MCP, the entire process of invoking CLIs via agents could end up being brittle, as the output format could change and corresponding chained commands would break without notice. This goes for all of what we discussed earlier such as flags getting deprecated.

MCP allows for tools to describe themselves to agents through discrete, structured schemas.

{
  "name": "deploy",
  "description": "Deploy the application to a chosen environment",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "environment": {
        "type": "string",
        "enum": ["staging", "production"],
        "description": "Target environment"
      },
      "force": {
        "type": "boolean",
        "default": false,
        "description": "Skip safety checks"
      }
    },
    "required": ["environment"]
  }
}

Above is a showcase of capabilities that agents are discovering at runtime for a deployment tool. The agent can clearly validate inputs before execution and understand the overall contracts with ease. Furthermore, each MCP-enabled CLI is now instantly available to all agents that support MCP without any further bespoke integration work.

MCP tool descriptions can be derived directly from existing command definitions for the CLI. However, it's essential that your CLI has regression test suites using tools like bats-core that preserve its escape hatches and output formats in its releases prior to building an MCP server. Implementing the MCP protocol is made easier through frameworks such as fastMCP that make spinning up an MCP server straightforward. Depending on what your CLI manages, the MCP server can be configured as a stateless wrapper, run with an ephemeral in-memory store or a service backed with durable storage.

Once your MCP server is set up, sophisticated agent behaviors become the norm, such as orchestrating multiple tools in parallel, implementing workflows that chain different tools, and even evolving these workflows as more tools are made available to agents through MCP.

Tight Feedback Loops

AI agents operate in a fundamentally different way than humans. The cognitive currencies are different. While humans can afford to wait patiently to figure out their next steps or to rethink without disrupting their workflows, AI agents will consume computational resources to accomplish the same tasks, such as tokens, filling up their context windows, and facing timeout limits, too.

The solution isn't just speed, it's providing the right feedback, at the right time. Agent-CLI interactions require the following patterns, with each serving a different purpose.

Early validation

In your CLI, include commands that provide early signals if the agent is heading in the right direction, especially if the action is potentially destructive such as a deployment.

ansible-playbook nginx.yml --syntax-check
ansible-playbook nginx.yml --check --diff

In the above snippet, --syntax-check parses the Ansible playbook and resolves variables/roles to ensure there are no syntax errors. The command with the flags, --check and --diff, is a dry run operation that showcases exactly what changes are to be made if there are any, with a corresponding semantic exit code. This provides a clear signal to an agent making the operation if it's safe to proceed.

Progress Reporting

Long-running tasks initiated by agents need a way to report progress, otherwise they would just appear hung. An example of this would be event streaming from AWS CloudFormation. An agent can read the output, detect failures early, and even estimate when to interrupt itself based on how deployments are progressing. Furthermore, it's important that the streams CLI writes remain consistent, because AI agents often start tasks in the background and redirect output to files they can later inspect.

aws cloudformation describe-stack-events --stack-name app --output json

Graceful Termination

All CLI tools should incorporate best practices on abandoning operations while keeping state consistent. In essence, they should handle SIGTERM gracefully with appropriate cleanup measures. Agents should be able to rely on tools to work consistently, without which the overall efficacy of AI agents goes down. In this case, the quality of tools really does matter. The old adage of a bad workman blaming his tools for his poor workmanship really does not apply to AI agents. It is up to us to supply the right tools with correct behaviors baked into them.

Applying these patterns creates a significant compound effect. Faster feedback implies more iteration, better utilization of tokens by the agent and overall improvement in reliability in task completions by the agent.

Don’t Guess, Rely on Telemetry

Traditional CLI analytics try to find patterns in lifecycle management, which then moves into automation. Agents exhibit completely different usage patterns and they don’t gradually discover features. They adopt them all at once, and they may or may not read --help pages. They will, however, execute consistent flag combinations and follow highly repetitive chained commands in rapid succession. Telemetry is going to be more important than ever. In addition to usage patterns of humans and automation, we need to track usage by AI agents. The telemetry collected should be explicit and with user-consent. On the first run of the CLI, there should be a disclosure that showcases exact information that is collected (e.g., OS Version, base commands, flags, latency, anonymised installation ID, session ID, and timestamp) and an easy way to opt out such as MYCLI_NO_TELEMETRY=1.

export MYCLI_NO_TELEMETRY=1
mycli config set telemetry false

# Show current status
mycli telemetry status
# Telemetry: Disabled (MYCLI_NO_TELEMETRY=1)

Telemetry should be used to inform optimization priorities driven through discovery of usage patterns. Error rates from CI/CD systems should drive investment into structured outputs. Interruption or timeouts should drive performance improvements. Identifying consistent patterns should suggest better defaults.

A data-driven approach is even more central than before. The insights derived will promote better agent reliability, while still giving users control and transparency over what is emitted for telemetry.

The Multi-Audience Future: Humans, Automation, and Now AI Agents

Command line tools are increasingly becoming the universal API layer for human and Agentic AI collaboration. This collaboration reflects a significant shift in software development and should not be taken lightly. Tools that recognize this change will flourish in this reality. The command line has traditionally been about simple, composable interfaces that gave users the ability to achieve complex tasks. Now the tools need to evolve such that they can work with AI agents. The question is not will CLIs need to work with AI agents, the question is are they ready today?
aiSummary: 本文概述了命令行界面的五个关键设计模式，旨在确保其可靠性，并与新兴的 AI 代理和自主自动化时代相兼容。
oneSentenceSummary: 本文概述了命令行界面的五个关键设计模式，旨在确保其可靠性，并与新兴的 AI 代理和自主自动化时代相兼容。
summary: 本文为调整传统命令行界面以适应新兴的 AI 代理生态系统提供了全面的指南。文章强调，精心设计的命令行界面对于人类用户和自主代理都至关重要，并提倡确定性行为和可靠的交互。作者提出了五个关键的设计原则：首先，加入“应急方案”（显式标志、环境变量、语义退出码）来禁用以人为中心的功能，以适应自动化需求；其次，将命令行界面输出格式视为具有稳定版本控制的应用程序编程接口协议，以防止工作流程中断；第三，优先采用模型上下文协议（MCP）来实现 AI 代理的动态能力发现；第四，通过早期验证、实时进度报告和优雅终止来加强反馈循环，以优化代理资源消耗；最后，通过收集明确的遥测数据来了解代理的使用模式，从而实现持续优化。正如所说，不要靠猜测，要靠遥测数据。文章强调，命令行界面正日益成为人类与自主 AI 协作的通用应用程序编程接口层，因此需要积极改进设计以适应这一趋势。

mainPoints.point: 为了支持 AI 代理，命令行界面必须提供“应急方案”，以禁用以人为中心的交互。
mainPoints.explanation: 诸如交互式提示或分页器之类的功能虽然对人类来说很方便，但可能会破坏自动化工作流程。显式标志、环境变量和语义退出码提供了对代理至关重要的确定性行为。

mainPoints.point: 应将命令行界面输出格式视为具有严格版本控制的稳定应用程序编程接口协议。
mainPoints.explanation: 对结构化输出的重大更改可能会灾难性地破坏自动化。定义显式模式并实施语义版本控制可确保向后兼容性以及包括 AI 代理在内的下游使用者的可靠性。

mainPoints.point: 采用模型上下文协议（MCP）进行动态 AI 代理集成。
mainPoints.explanation: 模型上下文协议（MCP）允许 AI 代理通过结构化模式动态发现和理解命令行界面功能，从而无需容易出错的定制配置即可实现强大而灵活的集成。

mainPoints.point: 为 AI 代理与命令行界面的交互实施紧密的反馈循环。
mainPoints.explanation: 提供早期验证、实时进度报告和优雅的终止机制有助于代理有效地管理计算资源（令牌），并提高任务完成的可靠性。

mainPoints.point: 利用显式遥测来理解和优化 AI 代理的使用模式。
mainPoints.explanation: 传统的命令行界面分析不足以满足代理的需求。收集关于代理使用模式（例如，标志组合、链接命令、错误率）的明确的、用户同意的遥测对于数据驱动的优化和提高代理可靠性至关重要。

keyQuotes: 精心设计的命令行界面在 AI 代理时代至关重要，可以精确可靠地为人类用户和自主代理提供服务。
确保每个命令行界面命令都有一个机器友好的应急方案：标志、环境变量和有意义的退出码允许自动化兼容性，同时防止并帮助代理应对故障。
将命令行界面输出格式视为稳定的应用程序编程接口协议。对任何结构化输出的重大更改都可能破坏所有自动化工作流程；在每次更改时，进行语义版本发布并验证命令行界面输出模式。
从第一天起就优先采用 MCP 协议进行代理集成。这可以实现动态能力发现，从而使 AI 代理可以立即使用这些工具。
通过实时反馈和优雅终止来加强反馈循环，因为令牌是 AI 代理的货币。
命令行工具正日益成为人类与自主 AI 协作的通用应用程序编程接口层。
